text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 14 August 2019 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures following travel abroad; traumatic brain injury many years prior with residual mild cognitive issues; improving sleep pattern; osteoporosis under primary care follow‑up; previous alcohol dependence now in sustained remission; current smoker. 

Medication:  
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily  
- Carbamazepine (modified release) 400 mg twice daily  
- Topiramate 50 mg morning and 100 mg evening  
- Thiamine, Vitamin B compound, Calcium/Vitamin D supplements, Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Carbocisteine, Emollients as required 

I reviewed Bill in clinic today accompanied by his key worker, who has supported him for the past year. He reports a notable improvement in day-to-day steadiness and confidence. He is eating regularly and has resumed short walks to the local shops. On assessment, gait was slow but stable without aids, and he rose from the chair independently. There is no focal joint pain; tone is slightly increased on the right but non-painful through range. 

Seizure history and current status: His initial event was in April 2019 in Germany, arising from sleep. He awoke with jerking of the left arm and facial twitching. A second event occurred in Italy the following July 2019, once more during the night, lasting four minutes with similar semiology. Since returning to the UK, there have been no further events reported. He and his key worker attribute improvement to better sleep routines—bedtime and wake time are consistent, and caffeine is avoided after midday. Sleep hygiene improved; pattern appears more stable. No episodes of confusion on waking, no witnessed nocturnal movements, and no injuries. 

Examination: Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. Neurologically, speech was clear, cranial nerves intact, mild right-sided pyramidal pattern of tone without weakness, coordination preserved, and sensation grossly intact. Cognitive screening was unchanged from prior review. 

Investigations: He has had routine bloods arranged by the GP last month (FBC, U&Es, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D) reportedly within acceptable limits; we will request the printout for records. He has not yet completed the community sleep assessment referral but feels current measures are helping. No recent imaging required given stable course. 

Plan:  
- Continue current antiepileptic regimen without change.  
- Reinforce sleep hygiene strategies, which appear to be contributing to stability.  
- Obtain recent blood results from practice and repeat only if clinically indicated.  
- Safety advice reviewed for nocturnal events and travel; he understands to seek review if events recur or change in character.  
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if further episodes occur. 

Overall, I am encouraged by the trajectory; he is clinically stable and functionally improving, with no further events since July 2019. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify seizure events explicitly stated: 1) April 2019 (Germany) and 2) July 2019 (Italy). Count = 2 seizures. Determine current status: The letter states no further events since July 2019; clinic date is 14 Aug 2019, so seizure-free interval is < 6 months, hence cannot label as seizure free. Define observation window for frequency: from first to last reported seizure (April 2019 to July 2019). Calculate duration in months: April→May = 1, May→June = 1, June→July = 1; total = 3 months. Compute frequency: 2 seizures / 3 months = 2 per 3 month. Consider multiple possible windows: since July to Aug is 0 per 1 month, but per rule select the highest frequency; thus 2 per 3 month.
seizure_frequency_number: His initial event was in April 2019 in Germany, arising from sleep. A second event occurred in Italy the following July 2019, once more during the night, lasting four minutes with similar semiology.

Quote issue flagged for seizure frequency
